text: Living high (2,500 m) and training low (1,250 m) for 27 days improves sea-level 3,000 m time trial performance by 1.1% in elite runners.
One-third of elite runners achieved personal best 3,000 m times after a 27-day live-high-train-low altitude camp, even when starting near peak seasonal fitness.
Live-high-train-low altitude training produces a 3% improvement in VO2max in elite runners.
Circulating erythropoietin levels nearly double within 20 hours of ascending to 2,500 m, confirming rapid hematological response to moderate altitude.
specific_value: EPO from 8.5 to 16.2 IU/ml at 20 hours

text: Hemoglobin concentration increases by approximately 1 g/dl over a 4-week live-high-train-low camp at 2,500 m.

The performance benefits of live-high-train-low altitude training are similar in magnitude and mechanism for elite runners as for sub-elite runners, even in athletes near maximal oxygen transport capacity.
High-intensity training should be performed at low altitude (around 1,250 m) during altitude camps to maintain training quality while gaining altitude acclimatization benefits from living at 2,500 m.
specific_value: live at 2,500 m, train at 1,250 m
